planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil in hills or rows; Canary melon (Juan Canario, Yellow Canary) is a popular commercial and home-garden melon throughout the Mediterranean and Latin America, known for its brilliant canary-yellow skin and very sweet, pale white-to-green flesh with a mild, honey-like sweetness distinct from other melon types.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in hills 4 feet apart or in rows 6 feet apart; Canary melon is an inodorus type that stores well on the vine and post-harvest — harvest when skin turns deep canary yellow and the blossom end yields to gentle pressure. Cucumis melo var. inodorus 'Canary' (Juan Canario, Yellow Canary melon) is a winter melon type grown extensively in Spain, Latin America, and the Middle East and sold widely in American Hispanic grocery markets; the medium to large (5–10 lb), elongated oval fruits have a striking, smooth, bright canary-yellow skin and white to pale green flesh with a delicate, honey-sweet flavor that is milder and less aromatic than muskmelon or cantaloupe types but refreshingly sweet and very appealing. The inodorus type means the ripe fruit has little fragrance from the exterior — you must taste to judge ripeness; the flesh is crisp at the center and creamy near the rind. An excellent heat-tolerant melon for Gulf Coast and Southern gardens that maintains flavor even in high temperatures; stores well at room temperature for 2–4 weeks after harvest.

common_name: Santa Claus Melon (Christmas Melon, Piel de Sapo)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Santa Claus melon (Piel de Sapo = 'Toad's Skin' in Spanish) is a late-season Spanish heirloom with extraordinary keeping quality — mature fruits store for 3–6 months at room temperature, traditionally used to provide fresh melon for the Christmas holiday season, which gives it both its nicknames.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun; Santa Claus melon requires 90–110 days to maturity, so start early or choose a long-season location; it is well-suited to Gulf Coast and California gardens. Piel de Sapo (literally 'Toad's Skin') is a Spanish winter melon variety widely grown throughout the Iberian Peninsula and used as the Christmas melon (melon de invierno) because its extraordinary keeping quality — 3–6 months at room temperature without refrigeration — allows harvest in October to be enjoyed fresh through December and January. The large (8–15 lb), elongated-oval fruits have mottled green and yellow skin with irregular markings resembling a toad's skin (the Spanish common name); the flesh is pale green to white-cream with a sweet, mild, refreshing flavor different from the aromatic intensity of muskmelon types. Sold in American markets as 'Santa Claus melon' and widely available in Hispanic grocery stores year-round due to its storage ability. Requires full sun and a long, warm season; sets fruit reliably in Gulf Coast and Western gardens.
sources: Spanish Ministry of Agriculture Heirloom Variety Registry

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Haogen is a celebrated Israeli melon developed at Kibbutz Ein HaMifratz, prized for its extraordinarily sweet, aromatic flesh of exceptional quality — considered by melon enthusiasts and chefs to be among the finest-flavored melons in the world, the Israeli equivalent of the French Charentais for concentrated sweetness and intense melon aroma.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in well-amended, well-drained soil in full sun; Haogen is ready to harvest when the skin transitions from green to orange-yellow in patches and the fruits slip from the vine with minimal pressure — like all aromatic melons, it deteriorates rapidly after peak ripeness. Haogen (also spelled Ogen) was developed at Kibbutz Ein HaMifratz in Israel and remains the most celebrated Israeli melon variety, known throughout the melon-growing world for its extraordinary flavor; the small to medium (1–3 lb), round fruits start with pale green skin that develops orange and gold patches and creeping yellow-green net as they ripen. The flesh is salmon-orange to greenish, with an intensely sweet, highly aromatic, complex melon fragrance and flavor with notes of honey and tropical fruit that many consider the finest melon eating experience available. Short shelf life after harvest (1–2 days at room temperature, 3–5 days refrigerated) means Haogen is fundamentally a garden and local market melon rather than a commercial shipping variety — its reputation rests on the incomparable quality of fruit eaten within hours of harvest. Widely grown in Israel, Netherlands, and by American melon enthusiasts.
sources: Israel Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Casaba is the longest-season common American melon (105–120 days), requiring the extended heat of Gulf Coast and Western summers for reliable ripening, but rewarding patient gardeners with large, golden-yellow, wrinkled-skinned fruits that store for months and have a distinctive mild, sweet, creamy-white flesh.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun; Casaba needs dry conditions at harvest to prevent splitting and rind cracking — reduce irrigation as fruits approach maturity. Casaba melon takes its name from Kasaba (now called Turgutlu), a city in western Turkey from which seeds were brought to the United States in the late 19th century; it is an inodorus winter-type melon with no surface netting and little external fragrance when ripe, unusual among American melons. The large (8–14 lb), round to slightly pointed-at-one-end fruits have deeply furrowed, wrinkled, golden-yellow skin and creamy-white to pale yellow flesh with mild, sweet, cucumber-like flavor that is less intensely aromatic than cantaloupe but refreshing and distinct. Casaba's extraordinary keeping quality (3–5 months at cool room temperature without refrigeration) made it historically important as a late-season melon available well after other varieties were finished; harvested in October it could remain in good condition through December. Excellent for dry summer climates (California, Arizona, Gulf Coast with good drainage); susceptible to powdery mildew in humid conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Persian melon is a large-fruited, heavily netted muskmelon type popular in California and the American West, producing large (8–15 lb), round, deeply netted, fragrant fruits with deep salmon-orange flesh of excellent sweetness — one of the standard commercial and home-garden melons for hot, dry Western climates.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun; Persian melon requires hot, dry conditions for best sweetness and is most successful in California, Arizona, Texas, and Gulf Coast gardens. Persian melon is a large-fruited, heavily netted muskmelon of Middle Eastern origin that became a California commercial standard in the early 20th century; the large (8–15 lb), round to slightly oval fruits have a thick layer of fine, closely woven gray-green netting over dark green skin that transitions to golden-green at maturity, with deeply aromatic, thick, deep salmon-orange flesh with a rich, spicy sweetness. Persian melon is rounder and larger than cantaloupes and has thicker, denser flesh with a more pronounced, spicy aromatic complexity than American cantaloupe types. It requires a longer season (90–100 days) and performs best in hot, dry conditions; California's Central and Imperial Valleys have historically produced most commercial Persian melons sold in Western markets. The thick rind and firm flesh give Persian melon better shipping quality than more delicate muskmelon types, and it stores 1–2 weeks refrigerated after harvest.
